title: Customer Service Workers and the Tax of Forced Calm
dek: You smile until your masseter muscle aches, swallowing the bile of unexpressed boundaries. This is the biological cost of forced calm, and your body is keeping the receipts.
bodyMarkdown:
You are standing behind the counter, or sitting in the headset, and the air in the room feels thin. The person across from you - or on the other end of the line - is raising their voice. They are demanding, they are cruel, they are treating you not as a breathing organism with a nervous system, but as a vending machine that has eaten their quarter. And what do you do? You smile. You soften your voice. You apologize. You swallow the hot, rising tide of your own biological defense mechanisms. 


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.233Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

This is the tax of forced calm. It is not just a psychological burden; it is a profound somatic event. When you are forced to suppress your natural defensive responses, your body does not simply forget. The energy of that suppressed anger has to go somewhere. It settles into the dense fascia of your neck. It tightens the masseter muscle of your jaw until you are grinding your teeth in your sleep. It pools in your gut, creating a slow, simmering acidity that no amount of antacids can truly quench. 

We are taught that professionalism means being unbothered. We are told that the customer is always right, and that our own boundaries are secondary to the transaction. But your body knows the truth. Your body knows that when someone violates your space, your dignity, or your peace, the natural, healthy response is anger. Anger is not a moral failing. It is a body-first messenger. It is the immune system of the psyche, rushing to the site of an injury to protect you. 

When you are forced to smile through the insult, you are overriding millions of years of evolutionary biology. You are telling your nervous system that its alarms are invalid. And so, the nervous system turns up the volume. 

Let us look at the anatomy of this forced calm. Notice what happens in your body when you have to apologize to someone who is actively harming you. Your diaphragm freezes. Your breath becomes shallow, trapped in the upper chest. The psoas muscle - the deep core muscle that connects your spine to your legs, the muscle that prepares you to fight or flee - contracts violently, but you do not move. You stay planted. You absorb the impact. 

This is what Dr. Gabor Maté speaks to when he discusses the biological cost of hidden stress. Maté observes that when we chronically suppress our authentic emotions to maintain attachment or, in this case, employment, we turn our biological defenses against ourselves. The anger does not disappear; it is redirected inward. It becomes inflammation. It becomes chronic pain. It becomes the exhaustion that settles into your bones at the end of a shift, an exhaustion that sleep cannot cure because your nervous system is still vibrating with unexpressed fight-or-flight energy. 

You might notice that your shoulders are permanently hitched up toward your ears, the levator scapulae muscles locked in a state of perpetual bracing. You might notice that your digestion is sluggish, because your body is constantly in a state of sympathetic arousal, shunting blood away from the digestive organs and toward the limbs that are not allowed to move. 

This is not pathology. This is biology. Your body is doing exactly what it is designed to do: it is trying to protect you. But the rules of the modern workplace demand that you ignore this protection. 

Consider the teachings of Resmaa Menakem, who reminds us that trauma and stress are not just cognitive experiences; they are lodged in the tissue. Menakem writes about the ways in which our bodies hold the historical and present-day impacts of systemic stress. For the customer service worker, the body becomes a sponge for the dysregulation of the public. You are not just managing your own nervous system; you are being forced to co-regulate with people who are entirely unregulated, and you are doing it without their consent or cooperation. 

When a customer yells at you, their nervous system is in a state of threat. Because we are social mammals, your nervous system detects this threat and responds in kind. But the script of your job requires you to override this response. You must present a calm, placating exterior while your internal landscape is a storm of adrenaline and cortisol. This dissonance - the gap between what your body is experiencing and what your face is allowed to show - is a profound source of somatic injury. 

Let us talk about the herbs that can support this specific kind of exhaustion. We are not talking about a gentle cup of chamomile to help you sleep. We are talking about deep, restorative nervines that can help rebuild a frayed nervous system. [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a profound ally here. It is not a quick fix; it is a slow, nourishing food for the nerves, helping to restore the myelin sheath and bring a sense of groundedness back to a body that has been vibrating at a high frequency for too long. 

[Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) is another vital plant for the customer service worker. It is specific for the kind of tension that manifests as a tight jaw, a tension headache, or the feeling that you are constantly bracing for the next impact. It helps to release the muscular holding patterns that develop when we are forced to suppress our anger. 

But herbs alone cannot undo the structural violence of forced calm. We must also begin to honor the anger itself. We must stop treating it as a liability and start recognizing it as a vital source of information. 

When you get off your shift, what do you do with the residual energy? Do you collapse onto the couch, scrolling through your phone to numb the vibration in your limbs? Do you pour a glass of wine to quiet the buzzing in your head? These are understandable coping mechanisms, but they do not complete the stress cycle. They do not allow the trapped energy to move through and out of the body. 

We need practices that allow the body to express what it was forced to suppress. We need to give the muscles permission to complete the actions they were primed for. 

### An Embodied Practice for Completing the Cycle

Find a space where you will not be observed or interrupted. This might be your car before you drive home, or your bedroom with the door locked. 

Stand with your feet hip-width apart. Feel the solid ground beneath you. Notice the tension in your jaw, your neck, your shoulders. Do not try to relax it yet. Just notice it. 

Now, bring to mind the most frustrating interaction of your day. The one where you had to smile while being spoken to like dirt. Feel the physiological response rising in your body. Notice the heat, the tightness, the urge to move. 

Instead of pushing it down, let it grow. Clench your fists. Tighten your jaw intentionally. Pull your shoulders up to your ears. Squeeze every muscle in your body as tight as you can, holding the tension, holding the breath for just a moment. 

And then, release. Let out a sound - a sigh, a groan, a growl. Shake your hands vigorously, as if you are flicking water off your fingers. Shake your arms, your shoulders, your legs. Stomp your feet on the ground. Let the body move in whatever way it needs to move to discharge the energy. 

Do this for as long as you need. When you feel the energy begin to settle, place one hand on your chest and one hand on your belly. Feel the rhythm of your breath. Feel the beating of your heart. Remind your body: *The threat is over. We are safe now. You are allowed to rest.*

This practice is not about "getting rid" of the anger. It is about honoring it. It is about letting the body know that its responses are valid, and that it does not have to hold onto the tension forever. 

The work of customer service is often deeply dehumanizing because it requires us to act as though we do not have human bodies with human needs and human boundaries. It requires us to pretend that we are unaffected by cruelty and disrespect. But we are affected. And our bodies bear the brunt of that pretense. 

When we begin to acknowledge the somatic cost of forced calm, we can begin to care for ourselves in a more profound way. We can stop blaming ourselves for the exhaustion and the pain, and start recognizing them as the natural consequences of an unnatural situation. 

We can also begin to set boundaries, even within the constraints of our jobs. We can practice the art of the "soft no" - the subtle ways we can protect our energy without risking our employment. We can learn to detach our self-worth from the interactions we have with dysregulated strangers. 

But most importantly, we can reclaim our anger. We can recognize it as the fierce, protective force that it is. We can let it remind us of our own dignity, our own worth, and our own humanity. 

The next time you are forced to smile through an insult, notice the heat rising in your chest. Notice the tightening of your jaw. And instead of judging yourself for it, or trying to push it away, simply say to yourself: *I feel you. You are right to be angry. I will take care of you when this is over.*

This is the beginning of somatic healing. It is the beginning of reclaiming your body from the demands of the marketplace. It is the beginning of honoring the deep, biological wisdom that lives within you. 

The tax of forced calm is high, but you do not have to pay it with your soul. You can keep your anger. You can keep your dignity. You can keep your body. 

And you can begin to heal. 

### The Anatomy of Suppression

Let us delve deeper into the specific somatic mechanisms of suppressed anger. When an emotion is generated, it is not merely a thought; it is a cascade of neurochemical events. The amygdala registers a threat - in this case, a hostile customer - and signals the hypothalamus, which in turn activates the sympathetic nervous system. Adrenaline and noradrenaline flood the bloodstream. The heart rate increases, blood pressure rises, and blood is shunted away from the digestive and reproductive organs toward the major muscle groups. 

This is the fight-or-flight response, a beautifully orchestrated symphony of survival. But in the context of customer service, you cannot fight, and you cannot flee. You must remain stationary and compliant. 

What happens to all that mobilized energy? It does not simply dissipate. It becomes trapped in the musculature. The body is primed for action, but the action is aborted. This creates a state of isometric tension - the muscles are contracting, but there is no movement. 

Over time, this chronic isometric tension leads to structural changes in the body. The fascia, the connective tissue that wraps around every muscle and organ, begins to thicken and harden in an attempt to support the constant state of contraction. This is why your neck and shoulders feel like concrete after a long shift. The fascia has literally remodeled itself to accommodate the chronic stress. 

Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal work on trauma, emphasizes that the body keeps the score. The physical symptoms of suppressed anger - the headaches, the digestive issues, the chronic pain - are not psychosomatic in the sense that they are "all in your head." They are the direct, physiological consequences of a nervous system that is constantly mobilized but never allowed to discharge its energy. 

Van der Kolk notes that healing requires more than just talking about the stress; it requires engaging the body. It requires movement, breath, and somatic awareness. We must learn to speak the language of the body, which is the language of sensation. 

When you are standing behind the counter, you can practice small, invisible acts of somatic regulation. You can focus on the feeling of your feet on the floor, grounding yourself in the present moment. You can practice slow, diaphragmatic breathing, which signals to the vagus nerve that you are safe, helping to down-regulate the sympathetic nervous system. 

You can also practice what somatic therapists call "pendulation" - shifting your attention between the area of tension in your body and an area that feels neutral or relaxed. If your jaw is tight, notice the tightness, and then shift your attention to your hands, or your toes, or the tip of your nose. This helps to break the trance of the stress response and reminds the nervous system that not every part of the body is under threat. 

These small practices will not change the fundamental nature of the job, but they can help to mitigate the somatic damage. They can help you to maintain a sense of agency and connection to your body, even in an environment that demands your dissociation. 

### The Role of Herbal Allies

In addition to somatic practices, we can call upon the wisdom of the plant world to support our nervous systems. We have already mentioned milky oats and skullcap, but there are other allies that can be profoundly helpful for the customer service worker. 

Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) is an adaptogen that helps the body to modulate its stress response. It is particularly useful for the kind of chronic, low-grade stress that characterizes customer service work. It helps to lower cortisol levels and supports the adrenal glands, which can become exhausted from the constant demand for adrenaline. 

[Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20), is another powerful adaptogen that has a specific affinity for the heart and the nervous system. It is uplifting and clarifying, helping to clear the mental fog that often accompanies chronic stress. It is a beautiful herb to drink as a tea before or after a shift, helping to create a sense of sacred space and protection. 

Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca) is a fierce, protective plant that is specific for the kind of anxiety that manifests as palpitations or a racing heart. It is a bitter nervine that helps to ground the energy and bring a sense of calm to the cardiovascular system. It is a plant that says, "I am here, and I will protect you." 

When working with these herbs, it is important to remember that they are not pharmaceuticals. They do not force the body into a state of compliance. Rather, they offer a template of balance and resilience that the body can learn from. They are partners in the healing process, not masters. 

### Reclaiming the Narrative

The narrative of customer service is one of subservience and self-effacement. We are told that our value lies in our ability to please others, to smooth over conflicts, and to absorb the dysregulation of the public without complaint. 

But this narrative is a lie. Your value does not lie in your ability to suppress your humanity. Your value lies in your inherent dignity, your capacity for connection, and your deep, biological wisdom. 

When we reclaim our anger, we reclaim our narrative. We stop seeing ourselves as victims of a toxic work environment, and start seeing ourselves as fierce, resilient beings who are navigating a difficult terrain with grace and courage. 

We can begin to advocate for ourselves, both individually and collectively. We can demand better working conditions, fair wages, and the right to set boundaries with abusive customers. We can support each other in our somatic healing, sharing practices and resources, and creating spaces where our anger is witnessed and validated. 

The tax of forced calm is a heavy burden, but it is not one that we have to carry alone. By turning toward our bodies, by honoring our anger, and by calling upon the support of our herbal allies and our communities, we can begin to heal the somatic wounds of customer service work. 

We can learn to stand in our power, even when we are standing behind a counter. We can learn to speak our truth, even when we are speaking through a headset. And we can learn to love our bodies, even when the world demands that we ignore them. 

This is the practice of somatic liberation. It is the practice of coming home to ourselves, one breath, one sensation, one moment of fierce, unapologetic anger at a time. 

### FAQ

**Q: I feel like if I let myself feel my anger, I’ll explode and lose my job. How do I manage this?**
**A:** This is a very common fear, especially when anger has been suppressed for a long time. The key is to separate the *feeling* of anger from the *expression* of anger. You can allow yourself to fully feel the somatic sensations of anger - the heat, the tension, the energy - without acting on them in the moment. Acknowledge the anger internally: *I am furious right now, and that is valid.* Then, use grounding techniques like feeling your feet on the floor or taking a slow breath to regulate your nervous system enough to get through the interaction. Save the physical discharge of the anger for a safe space and time, like the embodied practice described above. 

**Q: My jaw is constantly tight, and I grind my teeth at night. What can I do?**
**A:** This is a classic symptom of suppressed anger and forced calm. The masseter muscle is one of the strongest muscles in the body, and it holds a tremendous amount of tension. In addition to the embodied practices for discharging anger, you can try gentle somatic massage. Use your fingertips to gently massage the jaw muscles, the temples, and the base of the skull. You can also try taking a few drops of Skullcap tincture before bed to help relax the nervous system and release muscular holding patterns. 

**Q: I’m so exhausted after my shifts that I can’t even think about doing somatic practices. Is there an easier way?**
**A:** Yes. Somatic practice does not have to be a long, complicated routine. It can be as simple as taking three conscious breaths before you start your car. It can be noticing the feeling of the water on your skin when you wash your hands. It can be drinking a cup of Tulsi tea and simply allowing yourself to stare at the wall for five minutes without looking at a screen. Start small. The goal is not to add another task to your to-do list, but to create tiny moments of connection with your body throughout the day. 

**Q: How do I deal with the guilt of being angry at customers who might just be having a bad day?**
**A:** It is possible to have compassion for someone else’s suffering while still holding a firm boundary against their abusive behavior. Your anger is not a judgment on their character; it is a biological response to a boundary violation. You can recognize that they are dysregulated and struggling, while also acknowledging that it is not your job to absorb their dysregulation. Your anger is simply saying, *This behavior is not acceptable.* You can hold both truths at once: they are suffering, and you deserve to be treated with respect. 

The reality of this work is that it demands a constant, low-level dissociation. To survive the shift, you must disconnect from the subtle signals your body is sending you. You must ignore the need to use the restroom, the need to drink water, the need to stretch your aching back. You become a floating head, a voice, a pair of hands scanning items or typing on a keyboard. 

This dissociation is a brilliant survival strategy in the short term. It allows you to function in an environment that is fundamentally hostile to your biological needs. But in the long term, it creates a profound sense of alienation from your own physical form. You begin to treat your body as a machine that needs to be fueled and maintained, rather than a living, breathing organism that requires care and attunement. 

To heal from the tax of forced calm, we must slowly, gently begin to reverse this dissociation. We must begin to invite our awareness back into our bodies, even when it is uncomfortable. We must learn to tolerate the sensations of stress and anger, rather than fleeing from them. 

This is where the teachings of Pema Chödrön can be profoundly helpful. Chödrön speaks of the practice of "staying," of not abandoning ourselves when things get difficult. When the anger rises, when the tension in the jaw becomes unbearable, our instinct is to distract ourselves, to numb out, to run away. But Chödrön invites us to stay with the discomfort, to breathe into it, to offer it our compassionate attention. 

When we stay with our anger, we begin to realize that it is not a monster trying to destroy us. It is a wounded animal trying to protect us. And like any wounded animal, it needs to be approached with gentleness and respect. 

We can begin to build a relationship with our anger, a relationship based on trust and mutual understanding. We can learn to listen to its messages, to honor its boundaries, and to channel its fierce energy into creative and protective action. 

This is not easy work. It requires patience, courage, and a willingness to feel things that we have spent a lifetime trying to avoid. But it is the only way to truly heal the somatic wounds of forced calm. It is the only way to reclaim our bodies, our dignity, and our humanity. 

So the next time you feel the heat of anger rising in your chest, do not push it away. Do not swallow it down. Welcome it. Thank it for its protection. And let it guide you back home to yourself.

---
